description: Production authentication for the economy-explorer is pinned to a pre-release beta of next-auth (declared ^5.0.0-beta.25, resolved 5.0.0-beta.31 in package-lock.json). next-auth v5 has been in beta for an extended period; betas make no API-stability or security-patch guarantees, and a `^` range on a `-beta.N` version resolves forward across breaking beta increments (beta.25 -> beta.31 already happened). This is the auth layer of a money-adjacent read surface, so churn or an unpatched auth regression here has outsized impact.
recommendation: Pin next-auth to an exact beta version (drop the caret: "5.0.0-beta.31") so beta bumps are deliberate, and track the v5 GA release to migrate off the beta line as soon as it ships. Do not upgrade blindly across betas without reading the changelog for auth-flow breaking changes.
verification_note: Snippet "\"next-auth\": \"^5.0.0-beta.25\"," matches verbatim at economy-explorer/package.json line 27. package-lock.json confirms it resolved forward to 5.0.0-beta.31, so the beta.25->beta.31 forward-resolution claim is accurate. next-auth is genuinely the auth layer (lib/auth/authjs.ts, middleware.ts, types/next-auth.d.ts). Depending on a floating pre-release for the auth dependency is a legitimate dependency-hygiene concern. Note the 'outsized impact' framing is mild overstatement: this is a read-only explorer over a recoverable game economy, and npm treats ^5.0.0-beta.25 as >=5.0.0-beta.25 <5.0.0 (it will not jump to 5.0.0 stable), limiting drift to beta increments. Still a fair minor as a hygiene item on an auth dep, not a downgrade to nit given it is the auth layer.

description: The vitest/vite toolchain (vitest 2.1.9, vite 5.4.21, esbuild 0.21.5 resolved in package-lock.json) is a full major behind current (vitest 3.x, vite 6.x/7.x). esbuild 0.21.5 specifically is subject to the moderate dev-server request-forwarding advisory GHSA-67mh-4wv8-2f99 (any website can send requests to and read responses from the esbuild dev server; affects esbuild <=0.24.2). It is a devDependency (transitive via vitest/vite), so it never ships in the Next.js production bundle and the exposure is limited to a developer running the test/dev toolchain — but the advisory is real and the version is fixed.
recommendation: Upgrade the test stack: vitest and @vitest/coverage-v8 to ^3.x (which pulls vite 6.x / esbuild >=0.25 and clears GHSA-67mh-4wv8-2f99). vitest 2->3 has minor config/API changes (workspace config, mock defaults) but this repo's vitest usage is standard `vitest run` with vite-tsconfig-paths, so migration is low-risk. Feed this to the remediation run; do not upgrade here.

description: mybatis-spring-boot-starter is pinned to 4.0.1 while the rest of the module targets Spring Boot 4.1.0 GA (root build.gradle.kts line 15, applied here via the org.springframework.boot plugin). The mybatis-spring-boot 4.0.x line is built and documented against Spring Boot 3.x; MyBatis has not published a starter line explicitly tracking Spring Boot 4.x. The jar resolves and the module builds under the current setup, but pairing an unmatched integration-starter major with a Spring Boot major bump is exactly the kind of silent-drift the module is otherwise careful about (see the lettuce note at lines 52-57). The same mismatch applies to the test starter (mybatis-spring-boot-starter-test:4.0.1, line 74).
recommendation: Confirm mybatis-spring-boot-starter 4.0.1 is officially supported on Spring Boot 4.1, and if a Spring-Boot-4-aligned MyBatis starter release exists, move to it; otherwise document the pin's Boot-4 compatibility the way the lettuce floor is documented so the coupling is auditable. Keep the starter and its -test variant on the same version.

description: The Hibernia Framework — the shared Paper foundation bundled (shaded) into every deployable plugin jar (treasury, business, treasury-api-plugin, chestshop) — is pinned in the version catalog to a moving `-SNAPSHOT`. A SNAPSHOT coordinate resolves to whatever the latest published snapshot is at build time, so two builds of the same monorepo commit can shade different framework bytecode into the release/ plugin jars. For a foundational library that is compiled into shipped artifacts this defeats build reproducibility and makes it impossible to tie a released plugin jar to an exact framework revision. (paper-api's `1.21.11-R0.1-SNAPSHOT` is not comparable — Paper only publishes the API under the -SNAPSHOT coordinate, so it is a fixed name, not a floating snapshot the way an io.paradaux snapshot is.)
recommendation: Cut a fixed hibernia-framework release (e.g. 1.2.0) and pin the catalog to it before any release build, so a given monorepo commit shades a deterministic framework version. If snapshot iteration is needed during development, keep the snapshot only on develop and require a fixed version for the develop->main release PR.

description: server/bot/package.json is an un-customised `npm init` stub: it declares no dependencies, points `main` at an index.js that does not exist in the directory (the directory contains only package.json), and carries the default failing test script and empty author/description/keywords. It contributes nothing to the dependency graph and no lockfile exists for it. As a dependency-manifest it is dead weight and could mislead an audit or tooling into treating server/bot as a real Node package.
recommendation: Either populate server/bot with its actual sources and dependencies, or remove the stub package.json if the bot is not a real component. If kept as a placeholder, add a description noting it is a stub so it is not mistaken for a live package.
